# Beads Workflow Context

> **Context Recovery**: Run `bd prime` after compaction, clear, or new session
> Hooks auto-call this in Claude Code when a beads workspace is resolved

# ЁЯЪи SESSION CLOSE PROTOCOL ЁЯЪи

**CRITICAL**: Before saying "done" or "complete", you MUST run this checklist:

```
[ ] 1. git status              (check what changed)
[ ] 2. git add <files>         (stage code changes)
[ ] 3. git commit -m "..."     (commit code)
[ ] 4. git push                (push to remote)
```

**NEVER skip this.** Work is not done until pushed.

## Core Rules
- **Default**: Use beads for ALL task tracking (`bd create`, `bd ready`, `bd close`)
- **Prohibited**: Do NOT use TodoWrite, TaskCreate, or markdown files for task tracking
- **Workflow**: Create beads issue BEFORE writing code, mark in_progress when starting
- **Memory**: Use `bd remember "insight"` for persistent knowledge across sessions. Do NOT use MEMORY.md files тАФ they fragment across accounts. Search with `bd memories <keyword>`.
- Persistence you don't need beats lost context
- Git workflow: beads auto-commit to Dolt, run `git push` at session end
- Session management: check `bd ready` for available work

## Essential Commands

### Finding Work
- `bd ready` - Show issues ready to work (no blockers)
- `bd list --status=open` - All open issues
- `bd list --status=in_progress` - Your active work
- `bd show <id>` - Detailed issue view with dependencies

### Creating & Updating
- `bd create --title="Summary of this issue" --description="Why this issue exists and what needs to be done" --type=task|bug|feature --priority=2` - New issue
  - Priority: 0-4 or P0-P4 (0=critical, 2=medium, 4=backlog). NOT "high"/"medium"/"low"
- `bd update <id> --claim` - Claim work
- `bd update <id> --assignee=username` - Assign to someone
- `bd update <id> --title/--description/--notes/--design` - Update fields inline
- `bd close <id>` - Mark complete
- `bd close <id1> <id2> ...` - Close multiple issues at once (more efficient)
- `bd close <id> --reason="explanation"` - Close with reason
- **Tip**: When creating multiple issues/tasks/epics, use parallel subagents for efficiency
- **WARNING**: Do NOT use `bd edit` - it opens $EDITOR (vim/nano) which blocks agents

### Dependencies & Blocking
- `bd dep add <issue> <depends-on>` - Add dependency (issue depends on depends-on)
- `bd blocked` - Show all blocked issues
- `bd show <id>` - See what's blocking/blocked by this issue

### Sync & Collaboration
- `bd dolt push` - Push beads to Dolt remote
- `bd dolt pull` - Pull beads from Dolt remote
- `bd search <query>` - Search issues by keyword

### Project Health
- `bd stats` - Project statistics (open/closed/blocked counts)
- `bd doctor` - Check for issues (sync problems, missing hooks)
- `bd doctor --check=conventions` - Check for convention drift (lint, stale, orphans)

### Quality Tools
- `bd create --validate` - Check description has required sections
- `bd create --acceptance="criteria"` - Set acceptance criteria (checked by --validate)
- `bd create --design="decisions"` - Record design decisions
- `bd create --notes="context"` - Add supplementary notes
- `bd config set validation.on-create warn` - Auto-validate on every create
- `bd lint` - Check existing issues for missing sections

### Lifecycle & Hygiene
- `bd defer <id> --until="date"` - Defer work to a future date
- `bd supersede <id> --with=<new-id>` - Mark issue as superseded
- `bd close <id> --suggest-next` - Show newly unblocked issues after closing
- `bd stale` - Find issues with no recent activity
- `bd orphans` - Find issues with broken dependencies
- `bd preflight` - Pre-PR checks (lint, stale, orphans)
- `bd human <id>` - Flag for human decision (list/respond/dismiss)

### Structured Workflows
- `bd formula list` - See available workflow templates
- `bd mol pour <name>` - Start structured workflow from formula

## Common Workflows

**Starting work:**
```bash
bd ready           # Find available work
bd show <id>       # Review issue details
bd update <id> --claim  # Claim it
```

**Completing work:**
```bash
bd close <id1> <id2> ...    # Close all completed issues at once
git add . && git commit -m "..."  # Commit code changes
git push                    # Push to remote
```

**Creating dependent work:**
```bash
# Run bd create commands in parallel (use subagents for many items)
bd create --title="Implement feature X" --description="Why this issue exists and what needs to be done" --type=feature
bd create --title="Write tests for X" --description="Why this issue exists and what needs to be done" --type=task
bd dep add beads-yyy beads-xxx  # Tests depend on Feature (Feature blocks tests)
```

## Answer

**[well-established]** Every listed UFO-A construct can be represented as RDF 1.2 data, including statements about class membership and statements about those statements.  
**[well-established]** RDF 1.2, OWL 2 and ordinary N3 do not themselves assign possible-world semantics to named graphs or triple reifiers; RDF 1.2 explicitly leaves such dataset semantics to an extension. [RDF 1.2 Concepts](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf12-concepts/), [RDF 1.2 Semantics ┬з10](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf12-semantics/#rdf-datasets), [N3](https://www.w3.org/TeamSubmission/n3/)

**[plausible]** All constructs are executable and decidable over a finite, explicitly enumerated Kripke frame if the engine adds world-indexed predicates, explicit negative membership or stratified negation, and integrity constraints.  
**[well-established]** Full reference-UFO semantics over arbitrary possible worlds and arbitrary identity criteria is not reducible to decidable OWL-RL plus ordinary Horn N3.

### Shared RDF 1.2 carrier

**[plausible]** A concrete encoding can use either named graphs or RDF 1.2 reifiers:

```trig
:w1 a ufo:PossibleWorld ; ufo:assertionGraph :w1g .
:w1 ufo:accessible :w2 .
:alice ufo:existsIn :w1, :w2 .

GRAPH :w1g {
  :alice rdf:type :Person .
}

:r1 rdf:reifies <<( :alice rdf:type :Person )>> ;
    ufo:trueIn :w1 .

:r2 rdf:reifies <<( :alice rdf:type :Person )>> ;
    ufo:falseIn :w2 .
```

**[well-established]** RDF 1.2 triple terms need not be asserted, and reifiers are deliberately suitable for describing situations in which propositions hold. [RDF 1.2 Concepts ┬з1.5](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf12-concepts/#section-triple-terms)

**[plausible]** The engine should compile either representation into:

```text
exists(x,w)       holds(x,T,w)       notHolds(x,T,w)
accessible(w,v)   subtype(T,U)       identityProvider(T,K)
```

**[plausible]** A finite-world profile must declare its world set and membership data complete; otherwise absence of `holds` cannot soundly mean `notHolds`.

### Exact extension needed

**[plausible]** The minimal extension to an OWL-RL+N3 engine is a finite-modal Datalog layer with:

- **[plausible]** quotation/reifier or named-graph compilation into `holds/notHolds/exists/accessibility`;
- **[plausible]** integrity-constraint heads such as `violation(...)`;
- **[plausible]** explicit negation, or stratified negation over a declared-complete finite world set;
- **[plausible]** witness checking for anti- and semi-rigidity;
- **[plausible]** range-restricted identity-criterion rules.

**[well-established]** OWL 2 RL reasoning is polynomial-time, while unrestricted N3 with existential rule heads does not inherit that bound; decidability requires a restricted Datalog or known decidable existential-rule fragment. [OWL 2 Profiles](https://www.w3.org/TR/owl2-profiles/), [Existential N3](https://www.cambridge.org/core/product/identifier/S1471068425000055/type/journal_article)

**[plausible]** SHACL-SPARQL is the simplest implementation for validation; finite stratified Datalog/N3 is preferable if proof-producing inference is required. [SHACL 1.2 SPARQL](https://www.w3.org/TR/shacl12-sparql/)

## Family verdict

**Partly.**

- **[well-established]** **Yes** as RDF 1.2 representation: all constructs, formulas, worlds and statements-about-statements can be carried without a reification barrier.
- **[plausible]** **Yes** as decidable executable validation over a finite, explicitly complete set of situations, using the extension above.
- **[well-established]** **No** as a semantics-preserving implementation of unrestricted reference-UFO modal quantification inside ordinary OWL-RL+N3.
- **[plausible]** Full arbitrary-world semantics requires a genuine quantified-modal or higher-order prover; that extension is implementable as a separate regime, but not generally decidable and not an OWL-RL materializer.

**[well-established]** Therefore the prior тАЬRDF cannot express statements about statementsтАЭ rationale is incorrect. **[well-established]** The remaining boundary is modal entailment and completeness over possible worldsтАФnot RDF reification.## Verdict

[well-established] Every listed UFO-A moment can be represented as RDF 1.2 data, including statements about statements. [well-established] RDF 1.2 triple terms denote propositions without asserting them, while `rdf:reifies` introduces a separately describable reifier; N3 additionally quotes whole graphs/formulae and supports rules over them. [RDF 1.2 Concepts](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf12-concepts/#section-triple-terms), [N3 specification](https://w3c.github.io/N3/spec/).

[well-established] The remaining limitation is not reification but inference strength: OWL-RL plus safe Horn-N3 can execute structural moment rules, whereas unrestricted UFO dependence and disposition semantics quantify modally over possible circumstances/worlds. [plausible] Those modal conditions can be operationalized decidably over an explicit finite world/event carrier; complete unrestricted first-order-modal reasoning is not decidable in general.

## Minimal executable N3 extension

```n3
# Inherence entails existential dependence.
{ ?m ufo:inheresIn ?b. }
=>
{ ?m ufo:existentiallyDependsOn ?b. }.

# A dependent present in a represented world entails its bearer/dependent there.
{
  ?x ufo:existentiallyDependsOn ?y;
     ufo:existsIn ?w.
}
=>
{ ?y ufo:existsIn ?w. }.

# A relator depends on every entity it mediates.
{
  ?r a ufo:Relator;
     ufo:mediates ?x;
     ufo:existsIn ?w.
}
=>
{ ?x ufo:existsIn ?w. }.

# Trigger satisfaction activates a disposition.
{
  ?d a ufo:Disposition;
     ufo:triggerCondition ?condition.
  ?condition ufo:obtainsIn ?w.
}
=>
{ ?d ufo:activatedIn ?w. }.

# Normalize manifestation direction.
{ ?d ufo:manifestsIn ?event. }
=>
{ ?event ufo:manifestationOf ?d. }.
```

[well-established] OWL 2 RL itself has polynomial-time standard reasoning problems and is designed for rule-engine implementation. [OWL 2 Profiles](https://www.w3.org/TR/owl2-profiles/#OWL_2_RL).

[speculative] If the semantics must create previously unknown manifestation events or possible worlds, use a guarded or weakly-acyclic existential-rule/chase fragment. [well-established] Arbitrary existential rules or unrestricted N3 rules do not preserve the termination/decidability guarantee of OWL-RL plus safe Datalog.

- [well-established] Bare RDF supplies the carrier, not UFOтАЩs temporal, intentional, causal, or deontic consequence relation; RDF explicitly anticipates separately defined semantic extensions and entailment regimes. [RDF 1.2 Semantics](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf12-semantics/#semantic-extensions-and-entailment-regimes).

## Family verdict

- [well-established] **UFO-B: yes at the representation level.** Events, participation, temporal parts, mereology, causation and situations all have direct RDF node/reifier encodings.
- [plausible] **UFO-BтАЩs ordinary executable fragment fits finite safe Datalog over explicit events, intervals and worlds**, giving decidable materialization; unrestricted fusion axioms or counterfactual causation require existential/FOL/modal extensions with weaker decidability.
- [well-established] **UFO-C: yes at the representation level.** RDF 1.2 reifiers and N3 formulae remove the alleged inability to represent propositional attitudes, commitments, claims and norms.
- [plausible] **A practical UFO-C consequence relation can use function-free stratified Datalog over quoted content**, with explicit world/context/time parameters; it is decidable on finite datasets and implementable in existing rule engines.
- [well-established] **Bare RDF 1.2 alone does not validate the full intended UFO semantics.** It does not natively make beliefs opaque, evaluate goals, create obligations, enforce role anti-rigidity, or interpret named graphs as worlds.
- [plausible] **Full fidelity to arbitrary quantified modal/deontic UFO axioms requires quantified modal/deontic FOL or an equivalent theorem-prover layer.** Such a layer is implementable, but validity is undecidable in general, so timeouts and incomplete proof search must be exposed.
- [well-established] Consequently, the corrected answer is **тАЬall listed constructs are statable; many useful fragments are decidably rule-executable; unrestricted full-UFO semantics is not guaranteed decidable.тАЭ**

The earlier axis-1 claims in [expressivity-analysis.md](/home/ec2-user/css/kernel/kernel-of-truth/docs/next/arch/expressivity-analysis.md:26), especially G7/G10, should therefore be narrowed from тАЬnot statableтАЭ to тАЬnot natively interpreted by RDF/OWL entailment.тАЭ[VERDICT][SYN] The correction stands: statements-about-statements are available. The remaining gap is not reification but context-sensitive semanticsтАФespecially quantifying over situations, enforcing cross-situation identity, and validating existential/cardinality conditions. The smallest practical closure is a safe N3 profile over quoted situation graphs, plus a finite validation layer.

## Capability toolkit

| Toolkit | Expresses | Limits |
|---|---|---|
| RDF 1.2 / RDF-star | [STD] Triple terms can denote unasserted propositions; asserted triples remain graph members. Reifiers can carry provenance, confidence, temporal or modal metadata. Triple terms are transparent and currently occur as objects. [RDF 1.2 Concepts](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf12-concepts/), [RDF 1.2 Schema](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf12-schema/) | [STD][LIMIT] Quotation does not assert the quoted triple and supplies no implication, rule, possible-world, or situation semantics by itself. |
| RDF 1.1 reification | [STD] A resource with `rdf:subject`, `rdf:predicate`, and `rdf:object` describes a statement token, so statement metadata is representable. [RDF 1.1 Semantics](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-mt/#reification) | [STD][LIMIT] The reification neither entails nor is entailed by the base triple; identical components need not identify the same statement occurrence. Verbose and weakly axiomatized. |
| N3 | [CG] First-class quoted graph terms, variables, implication, graph/list/math/string built-ins, scoped graph inspection and scoped negation are available. [N3 Language](https://w3c.github.io/N3/spec/), [N3 Semantics](https://w3c.github.io/N3/reports/20230703/semantics.html) | [CG][LIMIT] It is a Community Group language, not a W3C Recommendation. Unrestricted built-ins, graph construction, network access and scoped negation do not give general termination or portable implementation behaviour. |
| OWL 2 DL | [STD] Decidable description-logic axioms: class constructors, disjointness, qualified cardinalities, property chains, inverses, keys and equality. [OWL 2 Syntax](https://www.w3.org/TR/owl2-syntax/) | [STD][LIMIT] No native possible-world/situation parameter. Metamodeling uses punning: the class and individual readings of one IRI are semantically separate. It cannot quantify over predicates or quoted assertions in DL. |
| OWL 2 RL | [STD] Horn-like, forward-rule-friendly subset supporting scalable type/property/equality closure. [OWL 2 Profiles](https://www.w3.org/TR/owl2-profiles/#OWL_2_RL) | [STD][LIMIT] Restricted cardinality and class-expression positions; it does not provide arbitrary existential witness generation, modal reasoning or semantically connected higher-order typing. |
| OWL 2 EL / QL | [STD] EL supports large existential-rich taxonomies with polynomial standard reasoning; QL supports ontology-mediated query rewriting over large relational ABoxes. [OWL 2 Profiles](https://www.w3.org/TR/owl2-profiles/) | [STD][LIMIT] EL omits most negation, universal and cardinality machinery; QL deliberately restricts constructors for first-order rewritability. Neither supplies UFO modal/metamodel semantics. |
| SHACL | [STD] Closed-data validation of types, paths, qualified counts, value ranges, equality/disjointness, logical combinations and closed shapes. [SHACL 1.2 Core](https://www.w3.org/TR/shacl12-core/) | [STD][LIMIT] Conformance is about the supplied data graph, not truth in every model or possible situation. Missing data may be a violation under SHACL while remaining merely unknown under OWLтАЩs open-world semantics. |
| SHACL-AF rules | [CG] Triple rules and SPARQL `CONSTRUCT` rules can monotonically add derived triples and can be conditioned by shapes. [SHACL-AF](https://www.w3.org/TR/shacl-af/) | [CG][LIMIT] It is a W3C Note/advanced feature, not an ontology semantics. General termination is not guaranteed, and rules still need an explicit convention for situations or named graphs. |
| SPARQL 1.2 | [STD] Queries asserted and quoted triples, datasets and named graphs; property paths express regular reachability; aggregation supplies `COUNT`, grouping and numeric summaries. [SPARQL 1.2 Query](https://www.w3.org/TR/sparql12-query/) | [STD][LIMIT] Query results are graph-relative. Paths are regular path matching, not general rules; aggregates count query solutions, not unnamed OWL-model individuals. RDF graph names are merely paired with graphs and need not denote them. [RDF 1.2 Concepts](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf12-concepts/#section-dataset) |

## UFO hard cases

| UFO feature | Existing coverage | Remaining requirement |
|---|---|---|
| Modal rigidity | [SYN] RDF/N3 can represent situations and quoted contents. | [LIMIT] A rule must quantify across accessible situations and condition membership on continued existence. The UFO definition is explicitly modal. [UFO overview paper](https://www.nemo.inf.ufes.br/wp-content/uploads/ufo_unified_foundational_ontology_2021.pdf) |
| Types of types | [SYN] RDF can state that a type instantiates another type; N3 rules may range over those terms. | [LIMIT] OWL DL punning does not connect the two semantic levels; cross-level constraints require rules or OWL Full-style metamodeling. |
| Identity criteria | [SYN] `owl:hasKey`, inverse-functional properties and `owl:sameAs` approximate operational keys. | [LIMIT] UFO identity principles are type-governed persistence criteria, not merely database keys; situation-relative persistence and criterion inheritance need explicit axioms. |
| Counting/cardinality | [STD] OWL DL expresses model-theoretic cardinality; SHACL and SPARQL count explicit values. | [LIMIT] OWL-RL cannot cover arbitrary qualified counts, while closed-data counts are not open-world existential consequences. |
| N-ary relators | [SYN] Not representationally hard: make the relator an individual and connect each participant by mediation roles. | [LIMIT] The hard part is existential dependence, distinct participants and qualified multiplicities. UFO requires a relator to mediate at least two distinct individuals. [OntoUML mediation](https://ontouml.readthedocs.io/en/latest/relationships/mediation/index.html) |

## Minimal extension: UFO-SN3

[PROP] **UFO-SN3** is a range-restricted, situation-scoped N3 profile layered over OWL-RL:

- [PROP] `holds(W,G)` relates a first-class situation `W` to a quoted N3 graph `G`; unlike a raw named graph, this relation has declared semantic force.
- [PROP] The entailment stratum is function-free positive Horn: no fresh blank nodes, graph-generating built-ins, remote dereferencing, negation or aggregation.
- [PROP] Variables may range over individuals, types, properties and situations, thereby supporting higher-order typing without OWL-DL punning.
- [PROP] Identity rules are equality-generating dependencies implemented by finite union-find/congruence closure.
- [PROP] A separate finite validation stratum admits stratified `notIncludes` and bounded distinct-value counts, but only for situations or predicates explicitly declared closed.
- [PROP] Lower cardinalities and anti-rigidity are validation obligations rather than invented existential witnesses.

[PROP] Representative rules:

```text
# Rigidity across accessible situations
Rigid(T) тИз Accessible(W,V) тИз ExistsAt(X,V)
тИз holds(W,{ X rdf:type T })
тЖТ holds(V,{ X rdf:type T }).

# Type-governed identity criterion
IdentityKey(T,P) тИз holds(W,{
  X rdf:type T. Y rdf:type T.
  X P K.        Y P K.
})
тЖТ Equal(X,Y).

# Relator projection
holds(W,{
  R rdf:type Marriage.
  R mediates X. R mediates Y.
}) тИз Different(X,Y)
тЖТ holds(W,{ X spouseOf Y }).
```

[PROP][LIMIT] Composite identity criteria compile to multi-property equality rules. Qualified cardinalities compile to bounded-distinctness constraints; minimum cardinality and anti-rigidity require an explicit witness or counter-situation in a closed validation scope.

## Verdict

[VERDICT][SYN] A small monotone ruleset alone is insufficient: it cannot detect missing existential witnesses or express anti-rigidity counter-situations. SHACL rules alone likewise lack modal context semantics, while raw named graphs lack a standardized denotation relation.

[VERDICT][PROP] UFO-SN3 is decidable and terminating for finite input under the stated restrictions: positive function-free Datalog reaches a finite fixpoint; equality only merges existing terms; bounded validation is finite model checking. For fixed rules, entailment remains polynomial in data size. Unrestricted N3 or unrestricted first-order modal satisfiability receives no such verdict.

[LOCAL] The fit with `sparq-reason` is unusually strong: it already exposes N3 and OWL-RL materializers and a range-restricted monotone RIF-Core front end ([lib.rs](</home/ec2-user/css/kernel/sparq/crates/sparq-reason/src/lib.rs:25>), [rif.rs](</home/ec2-user/css/kernel/sparq/crates/sparq-reason/src/rif.rs:1>)); formula-scope built-ins are tested directly ([n3_formula_scope.rs](</home/ec2-user/css/kernel/sparq/crates/sparq-reason/tests/n3_formula_scope.rs:1>)). The estate report confirms OWL-RL preprocessing and the SHACL/OWL verification gate ([sparq-estate.md](/home/ec2-user/css/kernel/kernel-of-truth/reports/sparq-estate.md:109)).

[LOCAL][LIMIT] The remaining implementation work is narrow but real: add a supported `holds(situation, quoted-graph)` index/head operation, equality-generating heads for identity criteria, and the closed validation stratum. The current RIF-Core layer deliberately forbids equality heads, negation and aggregation ([rif.rs](</home/ec2-user/css/kernel/sparq/crates/sparq-reason/src/rif.rs:29>)).

# The sparq vectorisation estate тАФ technical map & reusability assessment for the Kernel of Truth

**Scope.** Read-only survey of `/home/ec2-user/css/kernel/sparq` (crate `sparq-vectors`, plus
`sparq-canon`, `sparq-zk`, and the vector design records under `research/`), assessed against the
kernel programme's need: a **deterministic, training-free NSM-explication тЖТ vector encoder** with a
`(concept-hash тЖТ embedding)` table and a decode path back to consumable objects
(design doc: `/home/ec2-user/css/kernel/concept-gist/concept-hash-design.md`, esp. ┬з3тАУ┬з6, ┬з9a).

**Method note (empirical honesty).** Every claim below is grounded in code as it exists on disk,
cited `file:line`. Where the sparq design docs *plan* something the code does not do, that is said
explicitly. One tasking correction up front: `crates/sparq-serve/src/canon.rs` is **not** RDFC
canonicalisation тАФ it is SPARQL *query-text* cache-key canonicalisation (whitespace collapse +
optional variable alpha-renaming; `crates/sparq-serve/src/canon.rs:1-43`). RDFC-1.0 lives in the
standalone crate **`sparq-canon`** (`crates/sparq-canon/src/lib.rs:1-30`) and is consumed by
`sparq-zk` for commitments.

---

## 1. Deterministic vs trained: the exact split

`sparq-vectors` is architected so that **embeddings are normally produced out-of-process** and the
crate itself is a search/store/import surface (`crates/sparq-vectors/src/embed.rs:1-5`,
`src/structure.rs:10-17`). Almost everything *in-tree* is deterministic; the trained parts are
either external (remote embedding API) or an explicitly research-grade opt-in trainer.

### 1.1 Deterministic, training-free components (same input тЖТ same vector, no weights)

**(a) `HashEmbedder` тАФ deterministic hashed text projection** (`src/embed.rs:26-102`).
Test-only by declaration, but fully deterministic and cross-platform: per token (word +
character-trigram over whitespace-normalised text), a 64-bit **FNV-1a** hash XOR a seed feeds a
**SplitMix64** stream that contributes ┬▒1 to each of `dim` components; the accumulated vector is
L2-normalised (`embed.rs:41-72`, splitmix64 at `embed.rs:96-102`, default seed
`0x5354_5156_5645_4354` at `embed.rs:33`). Documented honestly: "It captures NO semantics ('car'
and 'automobile' are unrelated); do not ship it as a retrieval feature" (`embed.rs:21-25`).
Determinism is pinned by test (`embed.rs:679-687`). This is a signed random-projection over hashed
tokens тАФ the exact trick reusable for hashing *concept-hash* terminals into fixed-width lanes.

**(b) The typed-literal encoders (structure-aware P1)** тАФ pure functions keyed by datatype, "No
training, no graph state, no I/O" (`src/encode.rs:19-21`):

- **Datatype router** `route(datatype) -> Encoder` тАФ exact dispatch on the datatype IRI alone:
  numeric family (incl. all derived integer subtypes) тЖТ `Numeric`, `xsd:boolean` тЖТ `Boolean`,
  date/dateTime/dateTimeStamp/gYear тЖТ `Date`, everything else (strings, langStrings, IRIs) тЖТ
  `Other`/text lane (`encode.rs:149-166`; enum and taxonomy blocks carry their own tags,
  `encode.rs:114-129`).
- **Order-preserving numeric encoder** (`NumericEncoder`, `encode.rs:250-333`): (1) fit an
  empirical per-predicate range from observed values (`fit`, `encode.rs:267-271`; `normalise`
  currently linear over `[min,max]` with clamping, `encode.rs:286-305`); (2) map the normalised
  scalar through a **strictly-monotone thermometer/cumulative code**: `dim[i] = clamp((u тИТ i/d)┬╖d,
  0, 1)` (`encode.rs:343-350`). The provable invariant: L2 distance between codes is globally
  monotone in value distance тАФ "30 is nearer 31 than to 70, across the full range" тАФ checked by the
  exported metamorphic property test `metamorphic_monotone` (`encode.rs:768-801`), which a periodic
  Fourier code would fail by design (`encode.rs:44-48`). Order-preservation is exact under **L2,
  not cosine** (`encode.rs:50-55`).
- **Boolean sign encoder**: one reserved ┬▒1 dimension; `decode(slot) = slot >= 0.0` is an exact
  round-trip (`encode.rs:359-387`).
- **Date encoder**: temporal lexical тЖТ epoch-seconds scalar (`temporal_value`,
  `encode.rs:185-202`, XPath semantics via sparq-core `Timeline`, gYear handled directly) тЖТ the

**(c) The self-describing row layout тАФ `SchemaHeader` / `.spqs`** (`encode.rs:532-709`). A node

`(encoder tag, metric tag, offset, width, optional fusion weight)` (`encode.rs:469-513`).
Serialises to a deterministic little-endian byte string with magic `SPQS`, version 2
(`encode.rs:551-564, 629-644`); parsing is fail-closed (bad magic/version/tag/partition тЖТ `Err`,
`encode.rs:646-709`). The **metric-correctness guard** `check_euclidean` makes "cosine on a
non-Euclidean block" a detectable error, not silent corruption (`encode.rs:604-622`).

**(g) Deterministic renderers/readers**: `verbalize` produces the exact template string

### 1.2 Data-fitted (deterministic given pinned inputs, but corpus-dependent)

  per predicate (`encode.rs:258-271`). Same value + different fit corpus тЖТ different code. For a
  canonical kernel this means the fit breakpoints must themselves be pinned/content-addressed.
- `ScalarQuantizer` / `ProductQuantizer` (`src/quant.rs:1-52`): SQ per-dim ranges and PQ k-means
  codebooks are learned from the stored vectors (compression, not semantics).

### 1.3 Trained / external components

- **`RemoteEmbedder`** (`embed.rs:104-253`): an OpenAI-compatible `/v1/embeddings` client тАФ the
  production text lane is an **external trained model**; the crate only validates the response

  id, model version, or metric** тАФ an acknowledged gap
  (`research/specs/vector-genai-estate-recon.md`, "Key missing provenance").
- **`train.rs`** (`kge` feature): a minimal CPU DistMult/ComplEx trainer, deterministically seeded
  (SplitMix64 init + negatives, `train.rs:44-50`), but explicitly "research-grade, not a
  production training subsystem", existing only "to produce embeddings to measure"
  (`train.rs:9-19`).
- **`eval.rs`** (`kge` feature): a filtered link-prediction harness (MRR/Hits@k, 2├Ч2 closure ├Ч
  type-negatives ablation, multi-seed, paired deltas) (`eval.rs:1-80`). **Checked as tasked: no
  benchmark numbers have been produced/committed since the design doc.** The module states "No
  numbers live in this file or any committed doc. The harness *computes* them at run time"
  (`eval.rs:73-80`); a repo grep finds no committed KGE ablation figures тАФ only literature numbers

  posture stands: "No benchmark numbers appear anywhere in this document тАФ none exist yet"
  (`research/structure-aware-vectorisation.md:8-12`).

**Bottom line for Q1.** The entire *literal/structural* lane stack тАФ router, thermometer numeric,
boolean sign, date, enum codebook, unit normaliser, taxonomy ancestor-bag/depth, hashed

deterministic and training-free. The *relational/semantic* lane (what a node means beyond its

## 2. The decode direction: `grounding.rs`

`ground(graph, node, modality, cfg, introspection, store)` projects an **already-identified node**
into the object a consumer declared it needs (`src/grounding.rs:268-302`). Dispatch is by declared
`OutputType` тЖТ `Modality` (`grounding.rs:118-145`); ambiguous intent defaults to the exact,
re-checkable subgraph (`grounding.rs:47-51, 129-131`). Four modalities (`grounding.rs:99-187`):

1. **`Subgraph`** тЖТ `Vec<GroundFact>` тАФ `(predicate IRI, rendered object, is_literal)` triples in
   a **stable, dict-id-independent order** (sorted by predicate then object,
   `grounding.rs:386-427`). Minimality is ABSTAT-style: restrict to the predicates of the node's
   *effective (most-specific) type's* characteristic set, mined from `sparq-introspect`, with
   `rdf:type` always kept; fail-open to all facts when no pattern matches
   (`grounding.rs:434-506`).
2. **`TypedSubVector`** тЖТ `{values: Vec<f32>, blocks: Vec<Encoder>}` тАФ slices the node's stored
   `.spqv` row to only the requested encoder families using the `SchemaHeader` block spans
   (`grounding.rs:736-761`). This is the "return only the numeric lane / only the taxonomy lane"
   projection.
3. **`NlString`** тЖТ the deterministic `verbalize` passage, optionally extended with typed-value
   clauses (quantities, enum labels) under a char budget (`grounding.rs:310-349`).
4. **`TypedValue`** тЖТ exact typed slots: `Boolean(bool)`, `Number{value, datatype}`,
   `Quantity{value, unit}` (recognising the QUDT `qudt:numericValue`+`qudt:unit` shape,
   `grounding.rs:628-647`), `Enum{member, label}` тАФ "each value is the literal's own value", never
   a cosine match (`grounding.rs:534-581`). Optional cross-unit reconciliation converts
   known-unit quantities to canonical SI, conservative on unknown/compound units
   (`grounding.rs:649-676`).

Completeness is **profile-relative by contract**: ground over a closure-materialised graph and the
result is complete relative to that entailment profile only тАФ "No answer-completeness claim is
made" (`grounding.rs:31-45, 252-257`).

**Two honesty notes for the kernel.** (i) This is *node тЖТ object* projection, **not vector тЖТ
node inversion**. The vectorтЖТnode step in sparq is ANN search (`nearest_term`) followed by
grounding, and тАФ in the `neuro-symbolic` feature тАФ a **propose-then-verify** pipeline where a
deductive gate (SHACL + OWL inconsistency check) rejects candidates fail-closed
(`src/lib.rs:54-61`). (ii) Per-block decode exists only where the encoder is exactly invertible:
`BooleanEncoder::decode` (`encode.rs:380-387`) and `Codebook::decode` (`codebook.rs:123-133`)
round-trip; the thermometer numeric code has **no implemented decoder** (an inverse is
mathematically straightforward тАФ count saturated coordinates тАФ but is not in the code).

## 3. Where a `(concept-hash тЖТ embedding)` table lands

The storage seam is exactly two files:

**`store.rs` тАФ the `.spqv` table itself.** One f32 row per **dict term id (u32)**, one flat mmap
file: `magic "SPQV" | version=2 | dim u32 | count u64 | reserved | 24-byte graph fingerprint |
dense [count ├Ч dim] f32 | sorted (id u32, slot u32) index` (`src/store.rs:4-17`). Lookup is one
binary search plus a contiguous aligned slice (`store.rs:38-43`). Interfaces:
`create(path, dim)` тЖТ `put(id, &[f32])` тЖТ `with_fingerprint(graph)` тЖТ `finalize()`;
`open`/`open_from_bytes` for reads; `StreamingWriter` for O(1)-RAM builds (`store.rs:45-52,
163-226`); an optional delta sidecar (`.spqd`) for incremental add/remove/update behind the
`delta` feature (`store.rs:152-161`). The `SchemaHeader` travels as a sidecar byte string
(`encode.rs:538-543`).

**`import.rs` тАФ the bring-your-own-embeddings bulk path.** `ImportSpec { spqv_path, dim, ids,
binding }` plus either a NumPy `.npy` (v1тАУv3, 2-D, C-order, `<f4`/`<f8`) or a headerless flat f32
dump; **row `i` of the matrix is stored for `ids[i]`** тАФ the "row тЖТ dict-id contract"
(`src/import.rs:13-35, 100-110`). Validation is fail-closed before any proportional allocation;
the read is streaming (peak memory O(dim + index)) (`import.rs:37-59`). `ImportBinding::Graph`
binds the fingerprint (`import.rs:80-92`).

**How the kernel's table maps onto this.** A concept hash is a `urn:concept:<multibase>` URN тАФ
an IRI. Interned into a sparq `Graph`, it gets a u32 dict id, and the whole estate (store, ANN,
filtered search, `vec:nearest`/`vec:search` SPARQL predicates, `src/lib.rs:110-123`) works
unchanged: build the graph of concept records, run the deterministic encoder out-of-process (or
in-process), and `import` the matrix against the interned ids. Two caveats:

1. **Id-keying vs hash-keying.** The `.spqv` is keyed by *mutable-generation* dict ids, with the
   staleness contract of ┬з1.1(e). Concept hashes are immutable by construction, so the natural
   kernel key is the hash itself. sparq's own docs flag the term-keyed store as the right redesign
   (`fingerprint.rs:87-90`). Adapting `import.rs`'s rowтЖТid contract to a rowтЖТmultihash contract
   (sorted multihash index instead of sorted u32 index) is a small format fork, not a rewrite.
2. **No model/encoder provenance in the header.** The concept-hash design (┬з9a) wants tables
   "keyed by model identifier + version" as signed annotations; `.spqv` records neither
   (`research/specs/vector-genai-estate-recon.md`). For a *deterministic* encoder this becomes an
   encoder-version + profile-hash field тАФ which could live in the 12 reserved header bytes
   (`store.rs:11`) or the `SchemaHeader` sidecar.

## 4. Ontological structure as priors (`taxonomy.rs`, `shacl_priors.rs`) тАФ applicability to explication trees

**`shacl_priors.rs`** (feature `structure-shacl`) reads a parsed SHACL shapes model read-only and
emits per-predicate priors (`src/shacl_priors.rs:11-35`): `sh:in` тЖТ an exact `Codebook`;
`sh:datatype` тЖТ a router-confirmed encoder family (mixed-family lists resolve to `Other`,
`shacl_priors.rs:216-232`); `sh:maxCount 1` **or** `owl:FunctionalProperty` (read from the data
graph, not the shapes тАФ `shacl_priors.rs:234-254`) тЖТ `Cardinality::Functional` = **one
deterministic slot**, otherwise `Cardinality::Multi` = a **permutation-invariant pooled block**
(`shacl_priors.rs:49-60`). A predicate with no shape has no prior тАФ fail-open, never a guess
(`shacl_priors.rs:33-35`).

**Applicability to NSM explication records тАФ direct, with one gap:**

- The profile-1 grammar is *closed everywhere* (65-prime lexicon, closed role inventory, closed
  operator set with fixed arities, closed valency frames with req/opt slots, `refKind` enum,
  `semanticStatus` enum тАФ concept-hash-design ┬з4.1тАУ┬з4.6). Every one of these is exactly the
  **closed-enum codebook** case: exact slot-match encoding with a reserved invalid code, no
  training, no recall loss. Prime category, chart index (bounded integer тЖТ thermometer), frame
  type, operator, role тАФ all encode losslessly with the existing machinery.
- The **valency frames' req/opt slots** map onto the `Functional` cardinality prior: each role in a
  frame appears at most once, so each is "one deterministic slot" тАФ the structured-row discipline
  fits explication clauses well.
- `TaxonomyDag` + `EuclideanTaxonomyEncoder` transfer directly to the concept **dependency DAG**
  (explications reference other hashes; axioms include `subClassOf`): a concept's hashed
  ancestor-bag over its transitive definitional dependencies plus a depth-from-primes lane is a
  deterministic "position in the concept hierarchy" block, computable at mint time. The
  `GeometryGate` gives a principled, measured way to decide whether that DAG warrants a hyperbolic
  block. The `DisjointnessOracle` applies as-is since `cdef:rel` includes `disjointWith`
  (concept-hash-design ┬з5) тАФ giving an answer-safe mask over provably-disjoint concepts.
- **The gap:** explications are **ordered clause lists** (тЙд32 clauses) with cross-clause referent
  binding (┬з4.2, ┬з4.6). sparq's multi-valued prior is *permutation-invariant pooling* тАФ exactly
  wrong for ordered clauses тАФ and nothing in the estate encodes nesting or coreference. See ┬з6.

## 5. The ZK estate: commitment scheme and domain separation

**Scheme** (`crates/sparq-zk`): per-named-graph commitments over **RDFC-1.0-canonicalised**
content. Pipeline (`sparq-zk/src/commit.rs:1-13`): canonicalise the graph (via `sparq-canon`,
W3C rdf-canon test-suite validated, `sparq-canon/src/lib.rs:18-27`) тЖТ encode each canonical triple
to a BN254 field-element leaf in canonical N-Quads order тЖТ `C(G)` = one **Poseidon2 sponge** over
the leaf sequence. Term encoding (`sparq-zk/src/encode.rs:1-33, 45-75`):

- `Enc_t(term) = Poseidon2(type_code, blake3(bytes))` тАФ **Blake3 off-circuit, Poseidon2
  in-circuit**; 32-byte Blake3 digests map into the field by truncation to the low 31 bytes.
- **Domain separation, three layers:** (i) explicit **type codes** IRI=1 / Literal=2 / BlankNode=3
  as field-element separators inside `h_2` (`encode.rs:32-35`); (ii) literals hash their full
  canonical N-Triples token so datatype/langtag fold into the bytes (`encode.rs:19-22`, with a
  test pinning that `"1"`, `"1"^^xsd:integer`, `"1"@en` are distinct, `encode.rs:107-121`);
  (iii) the sponge's **length-bearing IV** gives per-leaf-count domain separation
  (`commit.rs:5-10`).
- **Blank nodes are graph-salted**: `Poseidon2(BlankNode_code, Poseidon2(salt_G,
  blake3(c14n_label)))`, salt drawn from the OS CSPRNG at ingest and recorded in the registry тАФ
  closing cross-graph bnode correlation (`encode.rs:4-15`).
- The commitment **method is a closed enum** recorded as a `zk:scheme` IRI; unknown methods fail
  closed, and the estate is candid that nothing is externally audited and the `dual-leaf` variant
  downgrades an in-circuit invariant to issuer honesty (`commit.rs:20-95`).

**Could concept-hashing and ZK commitments share discipline? Yes тАФ they already nearly do.** Both
pipelines rest on the same spine: extract a bounded record тЖТ validate fail-closed тЖТ RDFC-1.0
canonical N-Quads тЖТ hash with an explicit domain separator folded *inside* the digest. The
concept-hash design's profile header (`UTF8("urn:concept-def:1\n") тАЦ canonical bytes`, ┬з6 step 5)
plays the same role as sparq-zk's type codes and length-bearing IV: no payload can verify under the
wrong profile/shape. The differences are principled, not accidental: the kernel's records are
public and immutable, so it *rejects* ambiguous blank-node structure at the gate (duplicate-FDH
rule, trees only) where sparq-zk *salts* bnodes for cross-graph unlinkability; and the kernel uses
SHA-256/multihash for agility where sparq-zk uses Blake3тЖТPoseidon2 for circuit efficiency. A future
"ZK-provable concept kernel" (prove a vector was derived from a committed explication) could reuse
`sparq-canon` verbatim and re-target the leaf hash from `sha2-256` to the
`Poseidon2(type_code, blake3(┬╖))` composition without changing either design's canonicalisation or
domain-separation discipline. `sparq-canon` itself is the single most directly reusable artifact
here: it is exactly the RDFC-1.0-over-an-extracted-record engine the ┬з6 minting algorithm needs,
including the fail-closed HNDQ work-limit posture (`sparq-canon/src/lib.rs:44-49`).

---

## 6. Verdict: reusable for the kernel

### Reusable as-is (deterministic, tested, drop-in)

1. **`sparq-canon`** тАФ RDFC-1.0 canonicalisation, W3C-suite validated, with fail-closed handling
   of pathological inputs. The concept-hash minting pipeline (┬з6 of the design) can be built
   directly on it.
2. **The structured-row discipline**: `SchemaHeader`/`Block` with per-block encoder + metric tags,
   deterministic byte round-trip, fail-closed parse, and the metric-correctness guard
   (`encode.rs:532-709`). This *is* the record format for a partitioned concept vector.
3. **The exact closed-set encoders**: `Codebook` (one-hot + reserved invalid slot + decode
   round-trip) for primes/categories/roles/operators/frame-types/status; `BooleanEncoder`;
   thermometer `NumericEncoder` for bounded integers (chart index, restriction cardinalities,
   referent indices) тАФ with the fit breakpoints pinned in the profile rather than data-fitted.
4. **`EuclideanTaxonomyEncoder` + `TaxonomyDag` + `GeometryGate` + `DisjointnessOracle`** over the
   concept dependency DAG (┬з4 above) тАФ deterministic hierarchy position + measured-geometry
   decision + answer-safe disjointness masking.
5. **The hashing discipline**: FNV-1a/SplitMix64 seeded sign-projection (`HashEmbedder`) and the
   hashed ancestor-bag as the deterministic mechanism for folding *unbounded sets of
   content-addressed references* into fixed-width lanes; fixed-width, seedless, sorted-fold
   hashing per `fingerprint.rs` for artifact integrity.
6. **Storage + search estate**: `.spqv` store, `StreamingWriter`, `import.rs` bulk import,
   fingerprint binding, exact/HNSW/DiskANN search, filtered ANN, RRF fusion, and the
   `vec:nearest`/`vec:search` SPARQL surface тАФ a complete serving layer for a
   `(concept тЖТ vector)` table once ids are minted.
7. **The verification patterns**: `metamorphic_monotone` as the property-test template for encoder
   invariants; `ground()`'s modality dispatcher and `propose-then-verify`'s fail-closed deductive
   gate as the decode-direction architecture.

### Adaptable (small, well-understood changes)

1. **Hash-keyed rows.** Swap the `.spqv` sorted `(u32 id тЖТ slot)` index for a sorted
   `(multihash тЖТ slot)` index, or accept the id-keyed store plus a persisted concept graph and
   its staleness contract. sparq already names the term-keyed redesign as a follow-up
   (`fingerprint.rs:87-90`).
2. **Encoder/profile provenance in the header** (reserved bytes or the `.spqs` sidecar) to satisfy
   the design's model-identifier-keyed table requirement (┬з9a) тАФ trivially stronger here because
   the encoder is deterministic: the "model id" is a content hash of the encoder spec.
3. **Ordered composition.** Replace the `Multi тЖТ permutation-invariant pooling` rule with
   position-aware pooling for ordered clause lists (e.g. clause-index thermometer тКЧ clause lane),
   reusing existing lanes.
4. **Fit-free numeric normalisation.** Pin `NumericEncoder` breakpoints in the profile bundle
   (caps are already fixed constants тАФ record size тЙд 65,536, clauses тЙд 32, referents тЙд 32) so the
   corpus-dependence of `fit` disappears.

### Missing entirely (the kernel must build these)

1. **A recursive, compositional tree encoder.** Nothing in sparq encodes *nested structure* тАФ
   valency frames filled with sub-phrases, operator clauses over clauses, quote re-anchoring,
   referent binding тАФ into a vector. sparq's deterministic lanes are per-node/per-literal features;
   its compositional/relational semantics are delegated to *trained* KGE or external text models.
   The kernel's core encoder тАФ a deterministic fold over the explication tree (role-wise
   domain-separated binding/superposition, VSA-style, or any injective-enough composition
   operator) тАФ has no counterpart in this estate. sparq contributes the ingredients (domain
   separation discipline, seeded projections, closed-slot codebooks) but not the operator.
2. **Coreference encoding.** Indexed discourse referents (`cdef:bind`/`cdef:ref`) have no analogue
   anywhere in sparq's encoders.
3. **Graded semantic similarity from deterministic codes.** The estate is honest that hash lanes
   carry no semantics (`embed.rs:21-25`). A deterministic kernel's neighbourhood structure will
   reflect *structural overlap of explications* (shared primes, shared ancestors, shared frames) тАФ
   which is arguably the right notion for NSM, but is an untested hypothesis; no benchmark exists
   here or in sparq (eval harness present, zero committed numbers тАФ ┬з1.3).
4. **A non-Euclidean search kernel**, if the `GeometryGate` ever selects hyperbolic (sparq tags
   the block and refuses cosine on it, but ships no Poincar├й ANN).
5. **Inverse decoders for the numeric lane** (thermometer тЖТ value) тАФ feasible, unimplemented.

### One-line verdict

sparq supplies, tested and deterministic: the canonicaliser the concept-hash needs, the
partitioned-row format, the exact closed-set/numeric/taxonomy lane encoders, the hashing and
domain-separation discipline, and the full storage/ANN/decode serving layer тАФ roughly the entire
*periphery* of a deterministic explicationтЖТvector encoder. What it does not supply is the *centre*:
a deterministic composition operator that turns a nested, referent-bound explication tree into one
vector, and any evidence about the semantic quality of purely deterministic neighbourhoods. That
operator тАФ plus hash-keyed rows and pinned encoder profiles тАФ is the kernel's genuinely novel work.

## 0. Executive answer

A bridge is possible, but not as a total one-to-one mapping and not as a reduction of all UFO categories to NSM primes.

The viable form is a **hybrid**:

1. The NSM-derived kernel remains the grounded, human-auditable **lexical/explicational layer**.
2. Selected prime constructions supply evidence for, or anchor, some foundational distinctions: entity, event, action, agent, time, place, parthood, causation, desire and goal.
3. UFO supplies a separate **formal ontological layer**: types versus individuals, endurants versus perdurants, identity criteria, rigidity, existential dependence, qualities and modes as dependent particulars, relators, participation, and intentional/social structures.
4. An authored and endorsed bridge record connects an explication to a UFO classification or axiom. It must be allowed to return **underdetermined**.
5. UFO-grounded classifications then compile into the OWL-RL, Horn and policy strata already proposed for the world-model/rules engine.

The resulting relationship is therefore:

```text
NSM explication  тФАтФАevidence/paraphraseтФАтФАтЦ╢  bridge judgment
                                              тФВ
                                     authored + endorsed
                                              тЦ╝
                                      UFO/OntoUML profile
                                              тФВ
                                executable-fragment compilation
                                              тЦ╝
                             OWL-RL / Horn / validation / policy
                                              тФВ
                                              тЦ╝
                                  proof-carrying rules engine
```

The bridge provides part of the hoped-for тАЬbest of both worlds,тАЭ but only if тАЬbest of both worldsтАЭ means **two cooperating foundations**. It does not show that UFOтАЩs formal machinery is secretly reducible to the 65 primes.

**Recommendation:** pursue the hybrid. Treat direct mapping as a sparse alignment, build-up as explanatory evidence, and formal UFO commitments as an explicit sidecar with separate provenance. Do not infer UFO `Kind`, `Role`, `Phase`, domain/range, rigidity, or identity criteria automatically from the occurrence of NSM `KIND`, argument roles, or ordinary-language class descriptions.

## 1. Inventory: the Kernel-of-Truth side

### 1.1 Exact profile-1 prime inventory

The encoderтАЩs source of truth is [`encoder/src/lexicon.ts`](../../../encoder/src/lexicon.ts). It declares the NSM chart as `v20-2022`, fixes chart order as codebook-significant, and contains these exact 65 records:

| Category | Exact profile-1 exponents |
|---|---|
| Substantives | `I`, `YOU`, `SOMEONE`, `SOMETHING~THING`, `PEOPLE`, `BODY` |
| Relational substantives | `KIND`, `PART` |
| Determiners | `THIS`, `THE-SAME`, `OTHER~ELSE~ANOTHER` |
| Quantifiers | `ONE`, `TWO`, `SOME`, `ALL`, `MUCH~MANY`, `LITTLE~FEW` |
| Evaluators | `GOOD`, `BAD` |
| Descriptors | `BIG`, `SMALL` |
| Mental predicates | `THINK`, `KNOW`, `WANT`, `DON'T-WANT`, `FEEL`, `SEE`, `HEAR` |
| Speech | `SAY`, `WORDS`, `TRUE` |
| Actions, events, movement | `DO`, `HAPPEN`, `MOVE` |
| Location, existence, specification, possession | `BE-SOMEWHERE`, `THERE-IS`, `BE-SPEC`, `IS-MINE` |
| Life and death | `LIVE`, `DIE` |
| Time | `WHEN~TIME`, `NOW`, `BEFORE`, `AFTER`, `A-LONG-TIME`, `A-SHORT-TIME`, `FOR-SOME-TIME`, `MOMENT` |
| Space | `WHERE~PLACE`, `HERE`, `ABOVE`, `BELOW`, `FAR`, `NEAR`, `SIDE`, `INSIDE`, `TOUCH` |
| Logical concepts | `NOT`, `MAYBE`, `CAN`, `BECAUSE`, `IF` |
| Intensifier/augmentor | `VERY`, `MORE` |
| Similarity | `LIKE~AS~WAY` |

This broadly matches the stabilized contemporary NSM inventory. In particular, the repository correctly uses `IS-MINE`, rather than a generic possession prime `HAVE`; Wierzbicka describes the replacement of `HAVE` by the egocentric `MINE/BE MINE` form. The contemporary debate also commonly renders `PART` as the converse construction `HAVE PARTS`. **[LIT-BACKED]** ([Wierzbicka 2021](https://journals.rudn.ru/linguistics/article/view/26795))

### 1.2 The kernel is more than 65 labels

The bridge must target the complete profile-1 AST, not a bag of primes. The encoder adds a closed compositional metalanguage:

- 13 core roles: `agent`, `undergoer`, `experiencer`, `stimulus`, `addressee`, `topic`, `quote`, `complement`, `attribute`, `locus`, `possessor`, `instrument`, `comitative`.
- Four adjunct roles: `time`, `duration`, `place`, `manner`.
- Nineteen predicate frames, covering action/event, mental, speech, location/existence/specification/possession, life and death.
- Eleven operators: `NOT`, `CAN`, `MAYBE`, `IF`, `BECAUSE`, `WHEN`, `LIKE`, `AFTER`, `BEFORE`, `VERY`, `MORE`.
- Three top-level frames: `InstanceSchema`, `WhenTrue`, `RelationalSchema`.
- Five referent kinds: `SomeoneRef`, `SomethingRef`, `TimeRef`, `PlaceRef`, `ClauseRef`.
- Substantive phrases of the form `[determiner] [quantifier] [modifier]* head [restriction]`, with binding and coreference.
- Embedded clauses, quotations with re-anchored indexicals, temporal anchors and recursively resolved concept references.
- Caps of 32 clauses, 32 referents and structural depth 12.

This machinery is semantically important. For example, an event interpretation depends on the difference between `HAPPEN` as predicate, a clause referent, and an explication whose head denotes an event type. Agentivity depends on `DO` plus its `agent` role, not on the isolated `DO` vector. **[MEASURED]**

The deterministic vector is an encoding of this explication structure, with role/filler binding, ordered clauses and recursive concept references. It does not itself add ontological truth beyond the AST. **[MEASURED]** See [`docs/architecture.md`](../../architecture.md) and [`reports/deterministic-concept-vectors.md`](../../../reports/deterministic-concept-vectors.md).

### 1.3 Scientific posture

The repository already adopts NSM for its engineering propertiesтАФa small, closed, human-auditable vocabulary and grammarтАФnot as a settled proof of psychological universality or uniquely correct conceptual decomposition. Explications are treated as stipulative, endorsed artifacts. **[MEASURED]**

That posture should carry into the bridge. A bridge record says:

> тАЬGiven this endorsed explication and these additional modeling commitments, classify the concept this way.тАЭ

It should not say:

> тАЬThe prime text mechanically discovers the one true ontological category.тАЭ

## 2. Inventory: the UFO side

### 2.1 UFO-A: endurants and type structure

UFOтАЩs primary distinction is between:

- **Endurants**: individuals existing in time with all their parts and capable of qualitative change while retaining numerical identity.
- **Perdurants/events**: individuals unfolding in time by accumulating temporal parts.

A unique `Kind` supplies the identity principle governing which changes an endurant can undergo while remaining the same individual. UFO distinguishes types and individuals explicitly and formalizes instantiation and specialization in first-order modal logic. **[LIT-BACKED]** ([Guizzardi et al., тАЬUFO: Unified Foundational OntologyтАЭ](https://nemo.inf.ufes.br/wp-content/uploads/ufo_unified_foundational_ontology_2021.pdf))

Important UFO-A categories include:

- `ObjectKind`, `CollectiveKind`, `QuantityKind`
- `Subkind`
- `Role`, `RoleMixin`
- `Phase`, `PhaseMixin`
- `Category`, `Mixin`
- `QualityKind`
- `ModeKind`, including dispositions/capabilities
- `RelatorKind`
- qualities, modes and relators as dependent particulars
- formal, material, mediation and characterization relations
- identity, rigidity, sortality and dependence meta-properties
- extensional mereology, supplemented by more specific functional-part relations

`Kind` in UFO is rigid and identity-supplying. `Role` is anti-rigid and relationally conditioned. `Phase` is anti-rigid and intrinsically conditioned. A `Relator` is a reified, existentially dependent relational complex mediating multiple individuals. These are not merely labels for ordinary-language тАЬkind,тАЭ тАЬrole,тАЭ тАЬstateтАЭ or тАЬrelationship.тАЭ **[LIT-BACKED]**

### 2.2 UFO-B: events and participation

UFO-B covers:

- events/perdurants
- event and temporal mereology
- temporal ordering
- change and causation
- object participation in events
- the relation between endurant dispositions and their event manifestations
- atomic and complex events and, in fuller UFO work, processes

An endurant participates in a perdurant through a manifestation of a disposition inhering in that endurant. Thus participation is more formally committed than merely appearing as a grammatical argument of `HAPPEN`. **[LIT-BACKED]** ([UFO overview](https://www.inf.ufes.br/~gguizzardi/UFO-Story.pdf))

### 2.3 UFO-C: intentional and social entities

UFO-C builds on UFO-A and UFO-B and covers:

- agents and non-agentive objects
- beliefs, desires and intentions
- propositional contents and goals
- intentional actions
- social commitments and corresponding claims
- social roles and normative descriptions
- social relators

An agent can bear intentional moments and perform intentional actions. A goal is the propositional content of an intention. A social commitment inheres in one agent, depends externally on another, and is paired with a claim; such pairs can compose a social relator. **[LIT-BACKED]** ([Bringuente, Falbo and Guizzardi 2011](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/220541502_Using_a_Foundational_Ontology_for_Reengineering_a_Software_Process_Ontology))

### 2.4 BFO and DOLCE analogues

The bridge pattern is not UFO-specific:

| UFO | DOLCE analogue | BFO analogue |
|---|---|---|
| Endurant | Endurant/object | Continuant |
| Perdurant/event | Perdurant/event; state/process/accomplishment/achievement | Occurrent/process |
| Object | Physical/non-physical endurant | Independent continuant/material entity/object |
| Quality | Individual quality and quale/quality region | Specifically dependent continuant: quality |
| Mode/disposition | Non-qualitative dependent property; DOLCE extensions vary | Realizable entity: disposition/function |
| Role | Social/functional role, often via DnS extensions | Realizable entity: role |
| Participation | Participation relation | Participation/has participant |
| Time/space | Temporal and spatial qualities/regions | Temporal, spatial and spatiotemporal regions |

DOLCE is linguistically and cognitively oriented and therefore a natural secondary comparison target. BFO is a useful interoperability target for the programmeтАЩs OBO assets. UFO is the best primary target here because it supplies the richest formal account of `Kind`, `Role`, `Phase`, relators and intentional/social entities. **[PROVISIONAL]** ([DOLCE overview](https://www.loa.istc.cnr.it/index.php/dolce/); [BFO 2020](https://bfo-ontology.github.io/bfo-2020.html))

## 3. Direction 1: direct mapping

### 3.1 What тАЬmappingтАЭ can mean

A prime and a foundational category live at different levels:

- An NSM prime is an elementary meaning used in linguistic explications.
- A UFO category classifies entities or types and is constrained by a formal theory.
- Some primes are predicates or operators rather than entity categories.
- Some UFO notions are meta-properties of types, not ordinary first-order concepts.

Accordingly, the table uses:

- **A тАФ strong anchor:** a reliable bridge starting point, but not necessarily logical equivalence.
- **P тАФ partial/contextual:** overlap exists, but additional disambiguation or commitments are required.
- **├Ш тАФ no direct category mapping:** the prime belongs to logic, discourse or lexical semantics rather than a UFO base category.

### 3.3 Direct-mapping verdict

**Verdict on direction 1: PARTIALLY VIABLE. [LIT-BACKED/PROVISIONAL]**

A useful sparse alignment exists:

- `HAPPEN` тЖТ event/perdurant
- `DO` тЖТ action or agent participation
- `BODY` тЖТ physical endurant
- `PART` тЖТ parthood family
- `SOMEONE` тЖТ human-agent family
- temporal and spatial primes тЖТ foundational relations/regions
- `WANT` тЖТ desire
- `BECAUSE` тЖТ causation candidate
- quantifiers and logical primes тЖТ formal apparatus

But there is no defensible 65-row equivalence alignment. The main mismatches are:

1. lexical meaning versus ontological category;
2. predication versus reified entity;
3. ordinary `KIND` versus identity-supplying UFO `Kind`;
4. grammatical roles versus ontological roles;
5. natural modality versus UFOтАЩs explicit modal semantics;
6. loose parthood versus formal mereology;
7. descriptive relations versus reified modes and relators.

Direction 1 should therefore produce typed bridge assertions such as:

```yaml
target: ufo:EventType
relation: supportedClassification
strength: strong
evidence:
  - explication-pattern: "HAPPEN(...time..., ...place...)"
requires:
  - concept-denotes-occurrence
status: authored-endorsed
```

It should not emit unconditional equivalences such as:

```text
nsm:KIND owl:equivalentClass ufo:Kind
```

## 4. Direction 2: building UFO concepts from NSM compositions

### 4.1 Three senses of тАЬbuild upтАЭ

тАЬConstruct from NSMтАЭ can mean three different things:

1. **Reductive paraphrase:** give an intelligible explication using primes.
2. **Necessary-condition sketch:** use a prime construction to recognize some instances.
3. **Formal definition:** reproduce the identity conditions, modal commitments and inference behavior of the UFO category.

NSM can often do (1), sometimes support (2), and generally cannot do (3) without importing additional formal primitives.

### 4.3 Concrete build-up sketches

These are not proposed canonical NSM explications; they illustrate the bridge boundary.

#### Event

```text
something happened
it happened at one time
it happened in one place
because it happened, something was not the same after this
```

This can support classification as an event type. It does not define UFO event identity or temporal mereology.

#### Agent

```text
this someone can think about things
this someone can want something to happen
this someone can do something because this someone wants it
```

This gives a useful operational profile for agentive persons. It does not determine whether organizations or software systems count as agents.

#### Goal and intentional action

```text
someone wants something to happen
this someone thinks:
  "I will do something because of this"
after this, this someone does something
this someone does it because they want that other thing to happen
```

This closely approaches UFO-CтАЩs relation between desire/intention, goal and action. UFO still contributes reified intentional moments, propositional content and satisfaction relations.

#### Role

```text
at some time, someone is of this kind
this is so because this someone does something with another someone
before this, this someone was not of this kind
after this, this someone can be not of this kind
this someone is still the same someone
```

This distinguishes a role-like classification from a permanent biological kind. It establishes temporal contingency in one scenario, not UFOтАЩs universal modal anti-rigidity.

#### Disposition

```text
something can happen because something about this thing is like this
maybe it does not happen for a long time
when something else happens, this thing can do this
```

This is a strong commonsense explication of a capability. It does not reify a disposition, establish inherence, or identify a manifestation event.

#### Relator

```text
something happened because A and B did something
because this happened:
  something is so about A because of B
  something is so about B because of A
if A is not there, this cannot be so
if B is not there, this cannot be so
```

This explains why a marriage or employment relationship depends on participants and a founding event. It does not construct UFOтАЩs relator as the sum of mutually dependent qua individuals.

### 4.4 Build-up verdict

**Verdict on direction 2: EXPLANATORILY VIABLE, FORMALLY INCOMPLETE. [LIT-BACKED/PROVISIONAL]**

NSM composition can build intelligible profiles for:

- object-like enduring things;
- events, actions and movement;
- agents, beliefs, desires, intentions and goals;
- roles and phases at the commonsense level;
- dispositions at the commonsense level;
- simple temporal, spatial, causal and part-whole relations.

It cannot, without imported machinery, fully construct:

- type/individual stratification;
- sortality and identity provision;
- rigidity, anti-rigidity and possible-world quantification;
- formal identity criteria;
- complete mereological theories;
- inherence and existential dependence;
- qualities and modes as reified dependent particulars;
- qualia and quality structures;
- qua individuals and relator composition;
- participation as disposition manifestation;
- propositions, satisfaction and normative force;
- higher-order quantification over types and properties.

Calling the latter тАЬdefinitions in NSMтАЭ would conceal the fact that the formal semantics are being supplied by UFO.

## 5. Why the mismatch is principled

The mismatch is not simply missing vocabulary. It reflects different jobs.

NSM asks:

> What elementary meanings and grammatical constructions can make a complex lexical meaning intelligible without circular synonymy?

UFO asks:

> What kinds of entities and types must a model distinguish, and what axioms constrain their identity, dependence, persistence and relations?

Consequently:

- `KIND` communicates taxonomic similarity; UFO `Kind` imposes modal and identity conditions.
- `PART` communicates parthood; UFO supplies a mereological theory.
- `CAN` communicates possibility/capacity; UFO distinguishes modality from an inhering disposition.
- `DO` communicates action; UFO separates intentional action, agent participation and object participation.
- `WANT` communicates desire; UFO reifies an intentional moment with propositional content.
- `THE-SAME` permits sameness talk; UFO explains what makes sameness judgments valid for instances of a given kind.

This is why a bridge is possible but a collapse of one system into the other is not.

- rigidity and dependence annotations
- mediation, characterization and manifestation relations

Full reference-UFO commitments should be distinguished from the smaller subset executable in OWL. UFOтАЩs reference formalization uses first-order modal logic; an OWL implementation cannot preserve all of it. **[LIT-BACKED]**

#### Layer B3 тАФ executable compilation

Compile endorsed bridge records into separate artifacts:

1. **OWL-RL/RDFS entailments:** subclass, subproperty, domain/range, inverse, disjointness, selected property chains.
2. **Horn definitions:** safe rules not expressible or usable in the chosen OWL profile.
3. **Validation constraints:** identity-provider, forbidden combinations, cardinality and modeling checks. These detect bad models and must not silently become entailments.
4. **Policy/completeness rules:** local closed-world or covering assumptions, visibly labeled as policy.
5. **Reference-only commitments:** modal rigidity, full relator structure and stronger mereology retained for model validation or a richer prover, not falsely compiled into RL.

This matches the existing world-model architectureтАЩs separation of `owl-rl`, `horn-def` and `policy` proof regimes. **[MEASURED/PROVISIONAL]**

#### Layer B4 тАФ world model and rules engine

The ABox continues to hold persistent and prompt-scoped instance facts. The rules engine materializes only what the endorsed executable fragment licenses, retaining:

- source explication hash;
- bridge record;
- generated axiom;
- rule regime;
- premise facts;
- proof tree.

The host model consumes the resulting derivations through the existing retrieval, verification or direct-execution positions described in [`world-model-rules-engine.md`](world-model-rules-engine.md).

### 6.2 The compilation principle

The correct compilation direction is:

```text
explication
  тЖТ candidate ontological reading
  тЖТ authored bridge judgment
  тЖТ UFO model validation
  тЖТ executable axiom
```

It is not:

```text
prime occurrence
  тЖТ automatic OWL axiom
```

For example:

```text
KIND-frame(person)
```

does not license:

```text
Person rdf:type ufo:Kind
```

until an author supplies an identity principle and confirms rigidity/sortality.

Likewise:

```text
SOMEONE agent-of DO(...)
```

does not by itself prove that the concept being defined is an agent kind. The action may merely be something its instances sometimes participate in.

### 6.3 How this improves the g2/type layer

The provisional g2 proxy result warns that mechanically generated ordinary-meaning claimsтАФespecially domain/range and sortal typingsтАФare frequently over-committed, while the deterministic extraction machinery itself behaves as designed. Human adjudication remains pending. **[MEASURED within the repositoryтАЩs provisional evidence status; PROVISIONAL-ON-LLM-PROXY]**

A UFO bridge does not repair this by adding a richer vocabulary. It helps only if it imposes stricter discipline:

- `KIND` defaults to generic `Type`, not UFO `Kind`.
- A domain or range is emitted only when the explication states a necessary restriction, not merely a typical participant.
- Grammatical `agent` does not automatically create an ontological `Agent` class.
- Role-like and phase-like readings must identify their dependence or intrinsic condition and their identity provider.
- Unresolved alternatives remain an ambiguity set or `underdetermined`.
- Richer logic is not used to strengthen weak semantic evidence.

This produces a **less over-constrained type layer** because the foundational ontology acts as a set of proof obligations and refusal conditions, not as a larger palette for automatic typing. **[PROVISIONAL]**

### 6.4 Multi-ontology projection

UFO should be primary, but bridge records should target an ontology-neutral intermediate profile where possible:

```text
EntityProfile
  тФЬтФА EnduringEntity
  тФЬтФА Occurrence
  тФЬтФА DependentProperty
  тФВ    тФЬтФА Quality
  тФВ    тФФтФА RealizableCapability
  тФЬтФА RelationalComplex
  тФЬтФА AgentiveEntity
  тФФтФА SpatiotemporalRegion
```

Then maintain explicit projections:

- UFO for conceptual modeling, identity, roles, relators and social/intentional entities;
- DOLCE for linguistic/cognitive alignment and state/process/event distinctions;
- BFO for OBO and biomedical interoperability.

The projections will not be lossless. Recording that loss is preferable to pretending the upper ontologies are interchangeable. **[PROVISIONAL]**

---

## 7. What still has to be built

### 7.1 Bridge schema and governance

A versioned `kot-ont-bridge/1` record needs:

- source concept and explication hash;
- target ontology and exact version;
- target category/relation;
- mapping relation and strength;
- supporting AST clauses;
- added modeling commitments;
- counterexamples and ambiguity alternatives;
- author, endorsement and review status;
- executable projection, if any.

Bridge records should be annotations/sidecars unless the programme explicitly decides they are identity-bearing.

### 7.2 A pattern library

Author and validate a small library of patterns:

- occurrence тЖТ event candidate;
- intentional-doing тЖТ action candidate;
- desire/intention/goal;
- temporal-contingency + relational condition тЖТ role candidate;
- temporal-contingency + intrinsic condition тЖТ phase candidate;
- stable classification + identity criterion тЖТ kind candidate;
- `CAN` + manifestation pattern тЖТ disposition candidate;
- founded mutual dependence тЖТ relator candidate;
- magnitude/comparison тЖТ quality candidate;
- part plus functional dependence тЖТ component candidate.

Patterns propose or lint. They do not autonomously endorse.

### 7.3 Formal capabilities absent from profile-1

If full formal definitions are required, new machinery must be added outsideтАФor in a future version ofтАФthe profile:

- explicit equality and inequality predicates;
- instantiation and specialization relations;
- quantification over types/properties;
- modal necessity and possibility with a declared interpretation;
- inherence and existential dependence;
- mediation, characterization, manifestation and participation;
- proposition and satisfaction relations;
- normative notions such as obligation, permission, claim and discharge;
- formal mereological operators and sums;
- quality dimensions, values and measurement structures.

Adding these as hidden compiler assumptions would defeat the bridgeтАЩs transparency. They should be named UFO/formal primitives.

### 7.4 Reference semantics versus executable semantics

A compiler must declare its target:

- full reference UFO/FOL-modal;
- OntoUML validation constraints;
- gUFO/OWL;
- OWL-RL subset;
- Horn/Datalog;
- policy/closed-world rule.

No claim established only in reference UFO should be reported as an OWL-RL entailment.

### 7.5 Gold alignment set

Build a small independently reviewed corpus, initially perhaps 100тАУ200 concepts, stratified across:

- object/kind/subkind;
- role versus phase;
- quality versus mode/disposition;
- event/process/action;
- relator and ordinary binary relation;
- agent/intention/goal/commitment;
- deliberately underdetermined cases.

Measure:

- inter-modeler agreement;
- abstention/underdetermination rate;
- incorrect over-commitment;
- executable-axiom precision;
- whether UFO validation catches known g2-style sortal/domain/range errors;
- stability across ontology versions.

### 7.6 Grounding boundary

NSMтАЩs claimed grounding is grounding in shared human semantic competence. Encoding `GOOD`, `BODY` or `HAPPEN` as fixed symbols does not by itself give a machine sensorimotor grounding for them. The bridge improves semantic auditability and formal interoperability; it should not be described as transferring human grounding into the artifact automatically. **[LIT-BACKED/PROVISIONAL]**

---

## 8. Literature position

### 8.1 NSM and conceptual ontology

NSM explicitly presents concepts as compositions of semantic primes plus a universal syntax, with semantic molecules as intermediate reusable components. **[LIT-BACKED]** ([Goddard 2012](https://doi.org/10.1515/ling-2012-0022); [Wierzbicka 2015](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1002/9781118611463.wbielsi150))

The most directly relevant prior work located is Cliff GoddardтАЩs chapter **тАЬSemantic primes and conceptual ontologyтАЭ** in *Ontolinguistics*. It examines the ontology implicit in NSM, particularly its тАЬthing-likeтАЭ substantive primes. The volumeтАЩs introduction notes that NSM does not separate linguistic and conceptual entities in the way many ontology approaches do. **[LIT-BACKED]** ([book preview and contents](https://api.pageplace.de/preview/DT0400.9783110197792_A19082006/preview-9783110197792_A19082006.pdf))

This is important precedent for asking what ontology NSM presupposes. It is not evidence of an implemented NSMтЖТUFO, NSMтЖТDOLCE or NSMтЖТBFO formal alignment.

### 8.2 Lexical-resource to foundational-ontology mappings

There is established precedent for manually aligning lexical resources to foundational ontologies:

- тАЬSweetening WordNet with DOLCEтАЭ aligned WordNet upper noun synsets with DOLCE.
- Later work extended the mapping to verbs by linking verb synsets to nouns denoting perdurants, classifying them as event, state, process, cognitive event or cognitive state. The work emphasizes manual sense mediation rather than word-form equivalence. **[LIT-BACKED]** ([Silva, Freitas and Handschuh 2018](https://arxiv.org/abs/1806.07699))

This supports the general architecture:

```text
lexical/semantic representation
    тЖТ sense-sensitive bridge
    тЖТ foundational category
    тЖТ reasoning
```

It also warns against direct prime-label equivalence.

### 8.3 DOLCE and BFO

DOLCE is explicitly descriptive, linguistically/cognitively motivated and intended to provide general categories for integrating domain knowledge. It is therefore a natural comparative target for NSM. **[LIT-BACKED]** ([Borgo et al.](https://arxiv.org/abs/2308.01597))

BFO supplies the standardized continuant/occurrent, quality, role and disposition distinctions used by OBO ontologies. It is a natural interoperability target but is less directly aligned with NSMтАЩs commonsense linguistic orientation. **[LIT-BACKED]** ([BFO 2020](https://bfo-ontology.github.io/bfo-2020.html))

### 8.4 Negative literature finding

A targeted search found work on:

- NSMтАЩs implicit conceptual ontology;
- WordNetтАУDOLCE alignment;
- language-to-upper-ontology annotation;
- UFO/OntoUML grounding of domain models;
- BFO-aligned lexical and biomedical ontologies.

It did **not** locate a mature published system giving a systematic 65-prime NSM alignment to UFO, DOLCE or BFO, nor a proof that a foundational ontology can be reduced to NSM explications. This is a provisional negative search result, not an exhaustive bibliographic proof. **[PROVISIONAL]**

The proposed bridge therefore appears to combine known components in a relatively novel way:

1. closed NSM-derived AST;
2. content-addressed explications;
3. explicit foundational-ontology bridge records;
4. proof-carrying compilation into executable rules.

---

## 9. Risks and failure modes

| Risk | Consequence | Required control |
|---|---|---|
| Equating NSM `KIND` with UFO `Kind` | False rigidity and identity claims | Default to generic `Type`; require identity-provider review |
| Treating grammatical roles as ontological roles | Spurious agent, patient and role classes | Distinguish clause role from type meta-property |
| Compiling typical statements as necessary restrictions | g2-style domain/range over-constraint | Necessity rubric, counterexamples and `underdetermined` outcome |
| Treating `CAN` uniformly as disposition | Confuses ability, possibility and permission | Sense/pattern disambiguation |
| Treating every relation as a relator | Entity proliferation and false dependence | Require founding event, mediation and dependence evidence |
| Treating a paraphrase as a formal definition | Hidden imported axioms | Separate paraphrase, bridge commitment and formal target |
| Collapsing reference UFO into OWL | Lost modal/dependence semantics | Declare formalization target and loss profile |
| Automatic NL/ASTтЖТOWL authority | Recreates the ontology-authoring problem invisibly | Authored, endorsed bridge records |
| Refusing to abstain | Larger but less sound TBox | Three-valued classification and fail-closed compiler |
| Claiming machine grounding | Overstates what symbol definitions provide | Restrict claim to auditability, reference fixity and interoperability |

---

## 10. Feasibility summary

| Direction | Provisional verdict | What survives |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Direct mapping** | **Partially viable** | Sparse anchors and typed correspondences; no wholesale equivalence table |
| **2. Build UFO from NSM** | **Viable for reductive paraphrase and operational profiles; not viable as a complete formal reduction** | Events, actions, agents, goals, roles, phases and dispositions can be explained; modal identity/dependence/reification must be imported |
| **Hybrid** | **Recommended architecture** | NSM as meaning/evidence layer; UFO as explicit formal commitment layer; authored bridge as seam |
| Neither | Rejected as too pessimistic | Significant semantic overlap and established lexicalтЖТfoundational alignment precedents exist |

These are conclusions about the proposed bridge architecture only. They do not adjudicate the programmeтАЩs wider theses.

---

## 11. Recommendation

Proceed with a bounded hybrid prototype.

1. Keep the 65 primes and profile-1 AST unchanged.
2. Create `kot-ont-bridge/1` as a non-identity-bearing annotation initially.
3. Target UFO first, with explicit DOLCE and BFO projection fields.
4. Implement only five initial pattern families:
   - event/action;
   - agent/intention/goal;
   - generic type versus `Kind`;
   - role versus phase;
   - quality versus disposition.
5. Make `underdetermined` the default result.
6. Compile only endorsed mappings into the world-model TBox.
7. Preserve the rules engineтАЩs `owl-rl`, `horn-def`, `validation` and `policy` regimes.
8. Evaluate whether the bridge reduces false sortal/domain/range commitments relative to the current ╬а/g2 route.
9. Do not attempt relator synthesis, full modal rigidity or formal mereology in the first executable fragment.
10. Describe the outcome as **NSM-grounded explications interpreted through a UFO-grounded formal profile**, not as тАЬUFO derived from 65 primes.тАЭ

The central architectural insight is:

> NSM can explain much of what UFO categories mean, and can provide grounded evidence for selecting some of them. UFO is still needed to state what those selections formally commit the system to.

That seam is coherent, useful for the rules-engine direction, and honest about where the additional ontology enters.

**Method note (empirical honesty).** Every claim below is grounded in code as it exists on disk,
